query: As of early July 2026, what percentage of Russia's national oil-refining capacity is offline/disabled due to Ukrainian drone strikes?
answer:
I've completed the research. Here's a summary of the key finding:

**As of early July 2026, the answer depends critically on which metric is used:**

- **~42.74% (≈43%)** — the Ukrainian General Staff's cumulative claim (July 4, 2026), measured against Russia's total *designed/nameplate* refining capacity. This is the most widely circulated headline number.

- **~one-third (≈32–33%, or ~2.14 million b/d)** — independent analysts' (Energy Intelligence) estimate of *currently-offline operational* capacity, which is the more conservative and technically precise figure.

The two figures differ because the Ukrainian military measures cumulative damage against design capacity, while analysts measure current operational throughput. Refining runs hit a 21-year low (below 4 million b/d) in early June 2026. The campaign has caused an estimated $13.5 billion in industry losses since August 2025, and by July 2026 had struck all 11 of Russia's largest refineries — including the flagship Omsk refinery (440,000 b/d, ~12% of national output), which halted after a July 6–7 long-range strike, likely pushing figures even higher just after early July.

Full synthesis, 11 sources, and 10 structured data points have been delivered via the StructuredOutput tool.

sources.title: Ukrainian Defense Forces Disable 43% of Russia's Total Oil Refining Capacity
sources.snippet: The General Staff announced ongoing strikes have disabled 42.7% of Russia's total oil refining capacity, inflicting an estimated $13.5 billion in industry losses since August 2025.

sources.title: Defense Forces' attacks disable 42.74% of design capacity of Russia's oil refining - General Staff
sources.snippet: Systematic strikes disabled 42.74% of Russia's total designed oil refining capacity; General Staff reported July 4; Ukraine attacked eight refineries.

sources.title: Ukraine Disables Nearly 43% of Russia's Oil Refining Capacity — General Staff
sources.snippet: Ukraine's Defense Forces have disabled 42.74% of Russia's total oil refining capacity through sustained long-range strikes.

sources.title: Russian Refining: Drones Knock Out One-Third of Primary Capacity
sources.snippet: Nearly one-third, or 2.14 million b/d, of Russia's crude oil refining capacity is currently offline because of Ukrainian drone attacks. Refining runs in first week of June plunged below 4 million b/d, lowest in 21 years.

sources.title: Ukraine Drone Blitz Burns Moscow Refinery: One-Third of Russian Refining Goes Offline
sources.snippet: Approximately one-third of Russia's entire national refining capacity was already offline from months of accumulated strike damage; June 18, 2026 largest drone offensive on Moscow burned Gazprom Neft refinery, halted until at least 2027.

sources.title: General Staff says Ukraine knocked out 42% of Russia's refining and cost industry $13.5 billion since August 2025
sources.snippet: Ukrainian strikes idled 42.74% of Russia's oil refining; General Staff cumulative figure sits between two other assessments of the campaign's impact.

sources.title: Ukraine Hits All 11 of Russia's Largest Oil Refineries in Long-Range Drone Campaign
sources.snippet: With a strike on Omsk (>2,500 km from Ukraine's border), every one of Russia's 11 largest oil refineries has now been hit.

sources.title: Russia's Largest Oil Refinery Halts Production After Drone Attack, Sources Say
sources.snippet: Russia's largest oil refinery in Omsk halted operations following a Ukrainian drone attack; Monday's strike one of Ukraine's longest-range attacks.

sources.title: Drone Strike Shuts Russia's Largest Refinery as Fuel Shortages Worsen
sources.snippet: Gazprom Neft's Omsk refinery (440,000 b/d) halted after a Ukrainian drone strike damaged key processing units; Russia's largest gasoline producer.

sources.title: 2025–2026 Russian fuel crisis
sources.snippet: By July 2026, most of Russia's regions experienced fuel restrictions, affecting 50 million people (~35% of population); export ban and Euro-5 to Euro-3 quality reduction.

sources.title: Drone Strikes Halt Nearly 40% of Russia's Oil Refining Capacity
sources.snippet: Per Ciala data agency, as of September 28, 2025, 38% of primary oil refining capacity at Russian refineries was offline.

key_facts: Ukrainian General Staff claimed on July 4, 2026 that strikes disabled 42.74% (reported as ~42.7% or 'nearly 43%') of Russia's total DESIGNED oil-refining capacity.
Independent analysts (Energy Intelligence) put the CURRENTLY-OFFLINE operational figure at ~one-third, or ~2.14 million b/d, of Russia's crude refining capacity.
The two metrics differ: ~43% is the cumulative Ukrainian-military claim vs. design capacity; ~one-third (~32-33%) is the independent operational-throughput estimate.
Cumulative Russian refining-industry losses estimated at ~$13.5 billion since August 2025 (Ukrainian General Staff).
Russia's refining runs fell below 4 million b/d in early June 2026 — the lowest in 21 years (Energy Intelligence).
In the month before July 4, Ukraine struck 8 refineries and damaged 60+ storage reservoirs (58% petroleum products, 42% crude).
The Omsk refinery (440,000 b/d, ~12% of national output, Russia's largest and top gasoline producer) halted operations after a July 6-7, 2026 drone strike ~2,500-2,700 km from Ukraine.
By July 2026, all 11 of Russia's largest oil refineries had been hit by Ukrainian long-range drones.
The June 18, 2026 largest-ever drone strike on Moscow hit Gazprom Neft's refinery (~40% of Moscow's fuel), reportedly halted until at least 2027.
By July 2026, fuel restrictions affected ~50 million people (~35% of Russia's population); gasoline exports banned through July 31 and fuel standards lowered from Euro-5 to Euro-3.
Earlier reference point: Ciala data agency reported 38% of primary refining capacity offline as of September 28, 2025.

data_points.metric: Russia oil refining capacity disabled (Ukrainian General Staff claim, vs design capacity)
Russia crude refining capacity currently offline (Energy Intelligence analyst estimate)
Russia crude refining capacity offline (Energy Intelligence, absolute)
Russia refining throughput (first week of June 2026, lowest in 21 years)
Cumulative Russian refining-industry losses since August 2025
Russian refineries struck in month prior to July 4, 2026
